title: Vegan Diet Macros Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: 🩺 Vegan diet macros: 20% protein, 25% fat, 55% carbs. Calculate grams needed daily. Free plant-based nutrition calculator.
intro: A **vegan diet** excludes all animal products: meat, fish, dairy, eggs, and honey. The typical macronutrient distribution is **20% protein / 25% fat / 55% carbohydrates**, with protein sourced from legumes, grains, soy, nuts, and seeds. When properly planned, it's safe across all life stages (official position of the Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics 2016), but requires **mandatory B12 supplementation** and careful attention to iron, zinc, calcium, omega-3, and vitamin D. This calculator returns the grams of each macro based on your target calories and helps you combine legumes + grains to achieve a complete amino acid profile. It's the most restrictive form of vegetarianism and most criticized without proper planning.
keyTakeaway: Vegan: **20% protein + 25% fat + 55% carbs**. For 2000 kcal = 100g protein, 56g fat, 275g carbs. B12 supplementation is mandatory.
useCases: You're new to veganism and want to know your protein needs.
You want to reduce your environmental footprint.

example.title: Example: 2000 calorie vegan diet
example.result: Typical day: 300g lentils + 200g tofu + 1 cup quinoa + 2 tbsp chia seeds + 2 avocados + 5 servings fruits/vegetables.

explanation:
## What is a vegan diet

Excludes all animal-based foods. When properly planned, it is safe at all life stages.

## Recommended macros

| Macro | % of calories | Example (2000 kcal) |
|---|---|---|
| Protein | 20% | 100 g |
| Fat | 25% | 56 g |
| Carbohydrates | 55% | 275 g |

## Plant-based protein sources

| Food | Protein per 100g |
|---|---|
| Cooked lentils | 9 g |
| Tofu | 15 g |
| Tempeh | 20 g |
| Seitan | 25 g |
| Chickpeas | 8 g |
| Cooked quinoa | 4 g |
| Almonds | 21 g |

## Complete protein

- Legumes + grains (rice with lentils, hummus with whole wheat bread)
- Soy (naturally complete protein)

## Essential supplements

- **Vitamin B12**: 2000 mcg/week or 250 mcg/day.
- **Vitamin D**: if limited sun exposure.
- **Omega-3 (EPA/DHA)**: from algae-based source.
- **Iron**: monitor with bloodwork.
- **Zinc and calcium**: ensure adequate dietary intake.

## Common mistakes

1. Not supplementing B12.
2. Relying only on processed vegan foods.
3. Insufficient protein.
4. Ignoring iron intake.

## Related calculators

- [Vegan B12 Calculator](/calculadora-vitamina-b12-vegano)
- [Vegetarian Iron Calculator](/calculadora-hierro-diario-vegetariano)
- [Omega-3 to Omega-6 Ratio](/calculadora-omega-3-6-ratio)

Consult with a plant-based nutrition specialist.

faq.q: How much protein do vegans need daily?
faq.a: **0.8–1.2 g/kg of body weight** for sedentary people; **1.4–2.0 g/kg** for athletes. Aim 10% higher than omnivores.

faq.q: Is B12 supplementation really necessary for vegans?
faq.a: Yes, **always**. There are no reliable plant-based B12 sources.

faq.q: Can I eat a healthy vegan diet during pregnancy?
faq.a: Yes, with proper planning and supplementation. Supported by official ADA guidelines (2016).

faq.q: How do I get complete protein on a vegan diet?
faq.a: Combine legumes with grains throughout the day (rice + beans, lentils + whole wheat bread). Soy products are naturally complete.

faq.q: What are the best vegan protein sources?
faq.a: Tofu, tempeh, seitan, legumes, quinoa, nuts, and seeds. Mix and match for variety and complete amino acids.

faq.q: Do I need other supplements besides B12?
faq.a: Possibly. Monitor vitamin D, iron, zinc, calcium, and omega-3. Work with a plant-based nutritionist.
Yes. Prioritize protein intake (1.4–2.0 g/kg) from varied plant sources and strength training.

faq.q: What's the environmental impact of a vegan diet?
faq.a: Approximately **50% lower food-related carbon footprint** compared to a meat-heavy diet.

sources.name: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health
